title: IntrospectBench: A Cross-Domain Benchmark for Evaluating Step-Level Reasoning Introspection in Large Language Models
description: A multi-domain benchmark (Math, Logic, Commonsense, Code) with 1,200 problems that evaluates LLMs' ability to introspectively detect, localize, and characterize errors in chain-of-thought reasoning. Unlike existing benchmarks that focus on math-only errors or end-to-end self-correction, IntrospectBench provides controlled error injection with known positions and fine-grained metrics for reasoning introspection capabilities.
motivation: Current LLMs demonstrate strong reasoning generation but struggle with intrinsic error detection. Existing benchmarks like MWP-MISTAKE (math-only) and CorrectBench (evaluates self-correction methods) do not isolate the foundational capability of step-level reasoning introspection. There is no systematic evaluation of whether LLMs can detect errors in their own reasoning, identify error locations, or understand error types across different reasoning domains. This gap limits our understanding of LLM reliability and hinders development of self-correcting systems.
proposed_approach: IntrospectBench constructs a multi-domain dataset with controlled error injection: (1) Select problems requiring multi-step reasoning from GSM8K, LogiQA, CommonsenseQA, MBPP; (2) Generate ground-truth CoT solutions verified for correctness; (3) Inject controlled errors (6 types: calculation, logic, factuality, omission, misinterpretation, premature conclusion) at specific positions (early/middle/late); (4) Verify unambiguous error locations through human annotation. The benchmark evaluates three progressively difficult tasks: Error Detection (binary), Error Localization (step identification), and Error Characterization (type classification). Novel metrics include Introspection Score (composite), Position-Weighted Localization Score, and Domain Calibration Score.
hypothesis: LLMs will exhibit domain-dependent reasoning introspection capabilities, with stronger error detection in structured domains (mathematics, code) compared to open-ended domains (commonsense). Error detection performance will degrade as error position moves deeper in reasoning chains due to error propagation. Models will show an 'introspection gap'—better at detecting errors in other models' reasoning than their own.

confirming: Significant domain-dependent variation in introspection scores (>15% gap between best/worst domains)
Monotonic decrease in localization accuracy with error depth
Calculation errors detected more reliably than logic errors
Strong correlation (r > 0.7) between detection and localization performance
refuting: Uniform performance across all domains (suggesting domain-agnostic introspection)
Flat localization accuracy across error positions
No difference between self-generated and other-generated reasoning detection

related_work.paper: MWP-MISTAKE: Evaluating LLMs Ability to Handle Mistakes in Mathematical Reasoning
related_work.key_difference: Math-only domain (GSM8K, MATH, MATHBENCH). IntrospectBench covers Math, Logic, Commonsense, and Code.

related_work.paper: Can LLMs Correct Themselves? A Benchmark of Self-Correction in LLMs (CorrectBench)
related_work.key_difference: Evaluates self-correction methods (S1/S2/S3) with end-to-end outcomes. IntrospectBench isolates step-level introspection with controlled error injection.

related_work.paper: PRISM-Bench: Puzzle-Based Visual Tasks with CoT Error Detection
related_work.key_difference: Visual puzzle domain only with 1,044 problems. IntrospectBench covers text-based multi-domain reasoning.

related_work.paper: ProcessBench: Identifying Process Errors in Mathematical Reasoning
related_work.key_difference: Targets process reward model evaluation. IntrospectBench targets LLM introspection capabilities.

related_work.paper: Belief-R: Benchmarking LLMs' Belief Revision through Non-Monotonic Reasoning
related_work.key_difference: Evaluates belief revision with ~1,744 synthetic logical inferences using modus ponens/tollens. IntrospectBench evaluates step-level error detection in natural multi-step reasoning chains across 4 domains with novel metrics for detection, localization, and characterization.

related_work.paper: LogicBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating Logical Reasoning Capabilities of LLMs
related_work.key_difference: Covers 25 logical patterns including non-monotonic reasoning. IntrospectBench specifically targets step-level introspection with controlled error injection and localization metrics.
Focuses on multi-extension non-monotonic reasoning. IntrospectBench evaluates general reasoning introspection across natural language domains with fine-grained error analysis.

related_work.paper: Think²: Grounded Metacognitive Reasoning in Large Language Models
related_work.key_difference: Proposes a prompting framework (Planning-Monitoring-Evaluation). IntrospectBench provides a benchmark for measuring introspection regardless of prompting method.

related_work.paper: Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet
related_work.key_difference: Demonstrates limitations of intrinsic self-correction. IntrospectBench provides systematic measurement of these limitations across domains.
Compares holistic vs. stepwise scoring for failure detection on GSM8K and CoQA. IntrospectBench provides dedicated benchmark with controlled errors across 4 domains.

contributions: Cross-domain benchmark for reasoning introspection across Math, Logic, Commonsense, and Code
Controlled error injection methodology with known positions and verified annotations
Fine-grained evaluation framework distinguishing detection, localization, and characterization
Novel metrics: Introspection Score, Position-Weighted Localization, Domain Calibration
Empirical characterization of current LLMs' introspection capabilities and limitations

feasibility.compute: Well within budget: ~7.5 GPU hours total
feasibility.data: Building on existing datasets (GSM8K, LogiQA, etc.) with controlled modifications
